about.title: Hi, I'm Jabrecia!
about.content: I am a well-rounded front-end web developer and software engineer who keeps Jesus at the forefront of all that I do. I have a deep love for visualizations and visual design, and I am very passionate about code optimization. Using resources efficiently while ensuring the end product is visually appealing is my strong suit, and I would love to bring this dedication to your team or project! I also deeply enjoy graphics programming and I am pursuing graduate studies to further my knowledge and find new ways to use graphics programming across creative fields. Currently, my work focuses on front-end web development and UI/UX. My ideal roles would be entry level positions in front-end web development, software engineering, UI programming, and UX design. When I am not typing away at my computer, I enjoy watching movies, listening to music, reading, and gaming. Feel free to contact me using any of the links below! :)

projects.title: Audio Visualizer
projects.shortdesc: An interactive audio experience where you curate your visuals the way YOU want.

images.text: capture of the audio visualizer, there is a pink heart with a light and dark pink gradient in the backgroun, dark pink barsm and white sparkles. the background of the website is light pink with small purple dots
capture of the audio visualizer, this time it is full screen and no heart is on screen
capture of the audio visualizer, this time with no gradient in the background
capture of the audio visualizer, every element is present except for the heart

pagecontent: This audio visualizer utilizes the WebAudio API as well as the HTML canvas to display dynamic shapes and visualizations. I worked on this project for about one week in March 2023. The hearts, bars, sparkles, and gradient background are all drawn using the HTML Canvas. I alter the size of the hearts and the heights of the bars using the buckets of frequencies received from the WebAudio API. Larger hearts and higher bars correlate to higher frequencies, while smaller hearts and lower bars correlate to lower frequencies. The presence of these frequencies together creates a unique overlaying effect on the hearts and a casual drop off on the bars at the bottom.

The process of creating this Audio Visualizer involved sketching, planning, and implementation. In the sketching phase, I drew several designs for what I wanted the visualizer to look like and received some peer feedback. In the planning phase, I took time to figure out how to draw individual elements, like the heart and the sparkles. In the implementation phase, I combined the HTML Canvas and the WebAudio API to add movement and variability. Since I was using a lot of new technology for this project, I encountered some issues getting the separate APIs to interact the way I intended. Taking data from the WebAudio API and using it to alter the HTML Canvas proved difficult, but not impossible. The solution was patience, making sure I took the time to understand how the WebAudio API returned data (in buckets) and how to use those buckets to change the values being drawn on the canvas. In the end, the result was amazing and received recognition from my peers and my professors.

As of right now, this project is finished. I may return to it as I learn more about the technologies it uses, there are likely many places where things can be more efficient (for example, the way I draw the sparkles is very repetitive, so I'd like to make that more efficient). Overall, though, I'm happy with where things stand.

projects.title: The Black Game Dev Hub
projects.shortdesc: A simple website sharing resources for Black Game Developers to grow.

images.text: a web page with a white background, a logo saying BGDH is in the upper left corner, 'Black Game Dev Hub' in red, yellow, dark green, and light green text. the navigation bar is underlined with dark green, the text is in dark green
web page with a white background and a logo saying BGDH in the upper left corner, a list of Companies
a web page featuring the about page of the bgdh website and a photo of the website's creator (me!)

pagecontent: The Black Game Dev Hub (BGDH) features countless resources essential for any Black game developer, listing information from important game creators to follow to learning resources and tools. As the solo developer, I wanted to offer a place for inspiration as well as an opportunity for learning.

The process of creating this website involved creating a Figma mockup, developing several custom web components, then the final step of matching the design as closely to the mockup as possible. The Figma mockup was made for visual guidance alone, so there's no interactivity available there. In order to keep the website simple, I planned for the navigation to be reliant on a navbar that persists across pages, so I created a custom HTML component that I could reuse. I also made components for each link. Originally, I intended for each resource linked to contain an image and a small description. Due to time constraints, I was unable to include all of that data, so there is only a link for each resource as of right now. My greatest obstacle came with creating the components based on the information in each category's array. I walked myself in a few circles and overcomplicated things, but after taking a moment to step back and decompress, I came back to the project with a fresh set of eyes and was able to complete the website.

As of right now, this website is complete. In the future, I'd like to take out some of the redundancy in the project. The way I add all of the resources to each page is the same, but I have a different function for each page, which I'm not fond of. However, given the 24 hour time constraint, I like the place this website is in.

projects.title: Simple Graphics Engine
projects.shortdesc: A simple graphics engine created with DirectX11.

images.text: 5 3D shapes: a metallic cube, a wooden cylinder, a wooden corkscrew, a metallic sphere, and a wooden torus(donut). a pink sunset sky with clouds in the background. light reflects off of the metallic shapes. there are shadows underneath the shapes
5 3D shapes with an image of their shadow map to the left
the cube, cylinder, and torus have been rotated and stretched to show transformations, the menu that changes their properties is on the left

pagecontent: This simple graphics engine was created using DirectX11, and can render 3D objects within a simple scene. It supports lighting, shadows, materials, specularity, and a simple post processing effect (box blur). The development of this project was supported by my professor, and updates to the engine gradually became more self guided as time went on.

The process of creating this engine was iterative. We began with making sure simple shapes could be drawn, then slowly progressed to more and more complex shapes and later implementing core craphics principles (such as lighting equations). Most of the problems I encountered in developing this engine involved the many mathematical equations we needed to implement. At times, though I could understand what was being calculated, I couldn't easily wrap my head around using the equation to get the desired end result. Since this was a class project, I had many resources available to me, including my professor, so I was able to figure things out in the end. Patience was important, as well as communicating when issues arose.

Currently, this project is complete. In the future, I would like to expand upon the functionality of this graphics engine to allow for easier additions of 3D models, deleting of models, and more post processing effects (such as bloom and gaussian blur), as well as general performance improvements.

projects.title: InventHer Mobile App Mockup
projects.shortdesc: The initial mockup for the InventHer mobile app.

images.text: A pink background with text reading Welcome Back, and two areas to enter a username and password. The InventHer logo sits atop the page, Invent is in pink and Her is in black. A blue squiggly line is at the top of the page, a pink squiggly line is at the bottom. There is a pink login button at underneath the two text fields.

A pink background with text reading Join Us, and three areas to enter an email, username, and password. The InventHer logo sits atop the page, Invent is in pink and Her is in black. A blue squiggly line is at the top of the page, a pink squiggly line is at the bottom. There is a pink join button at underneath the two text fields.

A light pink background with a dark pink navigation bar at the bottom of the screen. The navigation bar has a calendar icon, an icon with three people (for community), an icon with a letter i within a circle (for information), and a single person icon (for user profile). The word Events is bold at the top of the page and written in pink. Two tabs are underneath reading Upcoming and Registered. Upcoming is filled in with dark pink. A filter button sits underneath on the left side, and on the right is a sort and calendar button. Three rectangles showing event information (name, time, location) as well as a View Event button for each are displayed underneath.

A light pink background with a dark pink navigation bar at the bottom of the screen. The navigation bar has a calendar icon, an icon with three people (for community), an icon with a letter i within a circle (for information), and a single person icon (for user profile). It's the same as the previous Events page, except the background has been slightly grayedout and a pop up sits on top that reads 'Welcome to the InventHer Events Page! Here you can view upcoming events, join events, and view your registered events. Events are a great way to get connected and get the most out of what InventHer has to offer!' Beneath the text, there is a pink button that says 'Let's Go!'

A light pink background with a dark pink navigation bar at the bottom of the screen. The navigation bar has a calendar icon, an icon with three people (for community), an icon with a letter i within a circle (for information), and a single person icon (for user profile). There is a 'Community' title with three group discovery cards underneath. Each card features a placeholder image, 'Group Name', 'Group Description', and a 'View Group' button. The right tab up top shows 'Discover' is selected.

A light pink background with a dark pink navigation bar at the bottom of the screen. The navigation bar has a calendar icon, an icon with three people (for community), an icon with a letter i within a circle (for information), and a single person icon (for user profile). There is a 'Community' title, and three cards underneath. Each group is represented by a square card with 'Group Name' and a 'Chat' button. The right tab up above shows that 'Your Groups' is selected.

A light pink background with a dark pink navigation bar at the bottom of the screen. The navigation bar has a calendar icon, an icon with three people (for community), an icon with a letter i within a circle (for information), and a single person icon (for user profile). A user's profile is displayed. The screen says 'Your Name', 'Joined 2025', and 'She/her/hers' next to a circular placeholder for a profile picture. Below this is a 'Personal Bio' section with placeholder text. A 'Notifications?' toggle switch is shown beneath and is toggled off. A gear icon for settings is in the bottom right corner.

pagecontent: This mockup was made for InventHer, a nonprofit aimed at increasing the number of women in leadership roles through mentorship, workshops, and building community. When I became a volunteer developer, they were planning on creating a mobile application for all members and prospective members to use. I was brought onto the team to help with this application, and the first step of the process was to create a mockup.

This mockup uses the original design of the InventHer website as design inspiration, though the site has since been redesigned. Though slightly outdated, the colors mirror InventHer's branding appropriately and utilize the same palette and visuals as the website did. As for the layout of the pages, I wanted the process of using the app to feel natural, so I used some common layouts I have seen across other applications, such as navigation at the bottom of the page, and the use of tabs on a page with different versions. The main goal here was maintaining consistency between the app and the website, as users will be interacting with both, and many who are currently a part of InventHer or working with InventHer will have grown accustomed to their branding via their website. Keeping that branding consistent ensures that InventHer has a clear identity no matter who is interacting with them or where they are interacting from. Another priority was reducing clutter and reducing the amount of movement throughout the app that the user needs to do. For example, on the Events and Community pages, there are two different tabs the user can interact with to see relevant information. This reduces the need for extra pages in the navigation bar at the bottom of the page. This also keeps both of those pages clean and keeps events the user registered for or their saved communities easily accessible.

The layout of this mockup will remain the same for the time being, but the colors and decor will be updated to align with InventHer's new branding guidelines. Once the mockup reaches a point the entire team is happy with, we will move forward with development of the app.

projects.title: Productivity App Lo-Fi Mockup (Mobile)
projects.shortdesc: The lo-fi mockup for the mobile version of a productivity app.

images.text: A dark-themed mobile app screen that features a hamburger menu icon and a search bar at the top. The main content area displays the text 'Nothings Here!' and 'Create your first task', centered on the screen. Below the text is a large circular plus icon, indicating an action to add a new item.

A dark-themed mobile app screen tthat displays a task list. At the top, there's a hamburger menu icon, a search bar, and filter icons. The tasks are organized into sections: 'Chores' (with tasks like Dishes, Laundry, Vacuum, Clean bathtub), 'Errands' (with Groceries, Dry Cleaning), and 'Volunteering' (with Shelter, Blanket Making, YMCA, which includes sub-tasks). Each task has a radio button, and each section has an 'Add Task' button.

A dark-themed mobile app screen showing a side panel menu. The menu includes sections for 'Saved Views' (Meetings, Home/Chores, Website/Database), 'Projects' (Home, School, Website, with 'Website' expanded to show Front-end, Back-end, Database, Other, and an 'Add Section' option), and 'Labels'. An 'Add New Project' button and 'Import'/'Export' options are also visible.

A dark-themed mobile app screen that features input fields for 'Task Name' with an 'Add Label' button, dropdowns for 'Project' and 'Section', and date pickers for 'Preferred Completion Date' and 'Deadline'. There's a 'Reminders' section with an 'Add Reminder' button, a text area for 'Description', and a 'Subtasks' section with an 'Add Subtask' button. Dropdowns for 'Size' and 'Priority' (with options None, Low, Medium, High) are present. A large 'Add Task' button is at the bottom. A back arrow and trash icon are in the top bar.

A dark-themed mobile app screen that displays a list of tasks under the 'Meetings' category, sorted by deadline. Tasks are grouped by date: 'Today', 'Tomorrow', 'Sunday Sept. 1', and 'Monday Sept. 2'. Each task entry includes a radio button, time (e.g., 10:45), and a description (e.g., Q1 Analysis). A hamburger menu icon and search bar are at the top.

pagecontent: This lo-fi mockup was made for a productivity app that I am helping a friend develop. The app is inspired by Todoist and includes several other quality of life changes inspired by GitHub project boards. In designing this lo-fi mockup, I wanted to prioritize pulling in the key features of both products that inspired the creation of this project in the first place.

One important feature to include was nesting. Projects contain sections, which contain tasks, and the user's screen can become very cluttered without the opportunity for nesting. The side panel was very important as it streamlines project, section, and task management, removing the requirement for the user to go into a menu to find settings and do such management there. One unique aspect of this project is the ability to create 'views', which will allow users to choose specific filters and save those to a 'view' for easier access. For example, if a user wanted to see tasks with a green label that are due within the next week, they could save those filters to a 'view' so that they can easily access those tasks. This removes the need for repeatedly applying the same filters to one's tasks. The design and layout of this page takes from a standard to do list app since the functionality is the focus there. The main goal for all of these pages is to keep things simple and reduce clutter, especially once the project is ready for mobile. I started with the mobile design first since scaling upwards tends to be easier than scaling down.

This mockup is complete on its own, though further updates and iterations will come during development. Also, the desktop mockup is a WIP and will also need to go through its own rounds of critique and updates. The mobile version can be viewed on figma right now. There are currently no interactions implemented, but the hi-fi mockup will include them if we determine that those interactions are necessary to assist our development.

projects.title: Questing
projects.shortdesc: Questing is a 2D isometric 16bit love letter to classic adventure games.

images.text: A small pixel art room with wooden furniture. The main character stands in front of a wooden counter with a pie in front of him. There is a shoe sticking out of the pie. The text above the character reads: Narrator: It's a shoe cobbler.
A large pixel art village. Each building and environmental element (trees, bushes, rocks) has a blue box on top of it displaying their collision boxes.

The image is zoomed in on a part of the pixel art village, focusing on three characters. The mayor, an old man with a gray beard, stands at the top. In front of him stands the main character, and to the main character's right stands Karusai, a blonde man with silver armor and red pants. The text box at the bottom of the screen includes a profile photo of Karusai, and the following text: All in a day's work.

The image is zoomed in on a part of the pixel art village, focusing on two characters: the main character, and to the main character's right, Karusai, a blonde man with silver armor and red pants. The text box at the bottom of the screen includes a profile photo of Karusai, and the following text: We shall reconvene tomorrow. I have...matters I must attend to.

pagecontent: Questing is 'A satirical love-letter to classic 16-bit adventure games, about a chosen hero who never wanted to be chosen.' A nod to games such as A Link to the Past, Chrono Trigger, and Secret of Mana, Questing combines the nostalgic feel of 16-bit adventure games with the witty humor of our lead, Ian Lynch. This is a fully volunteer project currently.

I joined this team after the demo was completed, so my work has been focusing on what comes after. Before beginning work on the game, I created a GitHub repo to make updates and reviews easier to manage. I also added a project board to the repo to help the team keep track of work that needs to be done. There have been many hurdles in coming onto this team since systems were set up prior to my joining the team, most notably the narration and cutscene systems (for myself, at least). However, we have all worked collaboratively to understand these systems and work with them. This is the first time I have joined a team in the middle of development, so this has been a wonderful chance to experience what it's like to learn about the setup of a project on the fly while also being flexible enough to handle any future changes. Currently, I'm adding in new dialogue and interactions that the team lead Ian Lynch has written. It's been super fun to learn how to use Godot and adapt to all of the new things I'm finding that the old development team created.
The project is still in development, and we are aiming to have the Demo 2.0 completed by 2027.

projects.title: HotSaus (hiihat Tech Fellowship)
projects.shortdesc: A social networking site that allows users to connect over the places, products, and services they love.
pagecontent: HotSaus is a social networking site that allows users to connect over the places, products, and services they love. Suggest a new restaurant to your friends, let your parents know about a new hairstylist in town, the possibilities are endless. HotSaus allows users to be honest about the things they truly love. Through it's unique receipts functionality, users can see real opinions from real people, and swipe through services with ease.

As a Tech Fellow, I was helping to develop HotSaus (formerly hiihat) and bring it to a stage that was presentable for acquiring funding. My work focused on building up the user facing side of the search engine. I created a positive user interaction experience while also ensuring that users on mobile and web devices will be able to easily and enjoyable utilize our product. Lots of communication was needed for this project since I was working with a backend developer, product designer, branding specialist, and the team that owns HotSaus. There were some bumps along the way, including some slight miscommunication between the developers, designers, and owners that caused some blockages. However, we were able to resolve the matter in an efficient manner that allowed us enough time to complete our final deliverable: a social feed. The social feed served as our 'final project' for the fellowship. Some technical issues I ran into came about when setting up new API endpoints in order to gather different user information for the feeds and ensuring styling and animation was consistent across devices. Tweaks were made, updates occurred, and, alongside the backend dev Kayley Tenney, the social feed was created.

My work on this project is done! I'm excited to see what the HotSaus team has planned in the future, as well as what future fellows will bring to the table. List of achievements: Developed React.js search component in alignment with brand guidelines, enhanced user experience by refining search item swipe animations with CSS animations, engineered user feed interactions and display, focusing on common user patterns on social sites
